coredata.dc:title: Electrochemical sensing of hydroquinone using green synthesized nitrogen-doped carbon dots

coredata.dc:description: Xenobiotic contaminants are dreadful to the environment. Hydroquinone is one of those toxins that need to be sensed using a nonenzymatic, environmentally friendly material. This was achieved by carbon quantum dots (CQDs) synthesized using hydrothermal supported by heteroatom doping using a bare glassy carbon electrode (GCE) electrochemically. The carbon dot’s largest active surface area of 0.13 cm2, better electron transfer capability, and doping enhance the functional groups and electrostatic interaction with a Limit of Detection (LOD) of 0.112 µM.

Hydroquinone
Active surface area
Heteroatom doping

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) of the modified electrodes
Electrochemical behavior of modified electrodes towards hydroquinone
Sensing by differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) and effect of variable scan rate
Concentration studies of hydroquinone using DPV
Anti-Interference, Repeatability, reproducibility, and stability studies

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a, b, c) HRTEM image of nCDs, (d) SAED pattern (e) lattice fringes, (f) FTIR spectra, (g) XRD pattern and (h) EDAX of nCDs.

(a and c) CV, and EIS of GCE, CDs, and nCDs recorded in a solution of 5.0 mM [Fe (CN)6] –3/-4 containing 0.1 M KCl, (b) Histogram of Ipa and Ipc of different electrodes, (d) CV @ different scan rates recorded in 0.1 M PBS, (e) DPV of nCDs illustrating the effect of individual concentrations ranging from (0.1–2.0) µM of HQ, (f) DPV @ 1000 µM of HQ.
Schematic representation and (a) Zeta potential, (b) XPS full spectra of nCDs, Deconvoluted spectra of (c) C 1 s (b) N 1 s (c) O 1 s.

head.ce:title: Electrochemical sensing of hydroquinone using green synthesized nitrogen-doped carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Advanced Nanomaterials and System Lab, Department of Materials Science, School of Technology, Central University of Tamil Nadu, Thiruvarur- 610 005, Tamil Nadu, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Materials Science, School of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Advanced Nanomaterials and System Lab, Department of Materials Science, Central University of Tamil Nadu, Thiruvarur 610 005, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Xenobiotic contaminants are dreadful to the environment. Hydroquinone is one of those toxins that need to be sensed using a nonenzymatic, environmentally friendly material. This was achieved by carbon quantum dots (CQDs) synthesized using hydrothermal supported by heteroatom doping using a bare glassy carbon electrode (GCE) electrochemically. The carbon dot’s largest active surface area of 0.13 cm2, better electron transfer capability, and doping enhance the functional groups and electrostatic interaction with a Limit of Detection (LOD) of 0.112 µM.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: An intensified release of different harmful substances into the ecological environment has resulted from the world's population boom and rapid industrialization. Di-substituted phenol-dihydroxy benzene positional isomer, hydroquinone (HQ) is widely used in many industries (cosmetics, dyes, medicines, oil refineries, textiles, plasticizers, and insecticides). In addition, HQ has detrimental effects on human health and the environment. Moreover, very low HQ concentrations may cause headaches, debility, hypoesthesia, vomiting, and kidney damage. It is mostly employed as a bleaching agent; nevertheless, prolonged usage may cause skin damage or severe allergies. Hence, it is classified as a trace xenobiotic pollutant. If not detected and measured, it will unintentionally impact the aquatic and human environments.

Quantifying the amount of HQ in wastewater bodies and marine life can be done in many ways mass spectrometry, fluorimetry, chemiluminescence and electrochemical methods, etc. Sophisticated equipment and well-trained technicians are needed for routine practical analysis [1]. Apart from the above factors, repeatability is the deciding and prime factor. The optimal technique should provide fast output, low cost, improved selectivity throughout a wider range, lowest LOD (comparison table shown in Supplementary Material (SM)), and superior sensitivity. The approach that meets the above requirements is electrochemical sensing.

Carbon-based materials attract great attention because of their excellent electron transfer, low toxicity, eco-friendliness, and economics. Zero-dimensional(0D) material, carbon quantum dots play a significant role in the field of sensing, MRI agents, and catalysts. Because of the promising performance, CQDs properties can be increased by heteroatom doping, which enhances the surface functional groups. Being 0D, it has the largest electrochemical active surface area.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (nCDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The nCDs were prepared from Nirmali seeds (Strychnos potatorum) using a simple hydrothermal method. After soaking the seeds for one day, the juice was prepared. 40 ml of the juice was treated at 240 ℃ for 5 h, and 4 ml of aqueous ammonia was added for heteroatom doping. Then it was filtered through a PVDF 0.22 µm pore size membrane and freeze-dried. The detailed electrode preparation is shown in SM.

The nCDs were first subjected to a High-Resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) to confirm the dot's nature and size. Fig. 1 (a, b, c, d) reveal the formation of spherical shape nCDs, and the amorphous nature was confirmed by the Selected Area Electron Diffraction (SAED) image. Fig. 1(e) possesses the d-spacing of 0.23 nm, calculated using FFT (Fast Fourier Transform) from the lattice fringes, confirming the graphitization nature at the (100) plane. The same was confirmed by the X-ray Diffraction pattern (XRD), which exhibits a broad peak of carbon at the (002) plane around 23.5˚ and peaks at 42˚ and 54˚, representing the presence of carbon with graphitic structure at the (100) and (004) plane. The many functional groups on the surface of nCDs provide them with exceptional water solubility. Fourier Transform Infra-Red (FTIR) spectroscopy was performed to elucidate the functional group information. This guarantee stretching vibrations of the O-H and N-H amine groups by a wide peak at approximately 3250 cm−1. Alkyl C-H, C≡C stretching vibrations, C=O, C=C vibrations, C-O-C @ 1243 cm−1 indicates the presence of epoxy groups, C-N stretching shown in Fig. 1(f). With the help of Energy Dispersive X-ray Analysis (EDAX) mapping [shown in Fig. 1(h)], nitrogen doping was ensured that enhances the sensing properties by creating more number of active sites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) of the modified electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: Properties of electron transfer at the interface were characterized by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy, especially by Nyquist plot in modified electrodes. It comprises two sections: one is charge transfer resistance (Rct) represented by a semicircle, and the second one is the diffusion-controlled process by linear area in high and low-frequency regions, respectively. The diameter of the semicircle of nCDs coated electrode is smaller than that of CDs and GCE-coated electrodes, which indirectly tells that the interfacial or charge transfer resistance is significantly low and shows better electron transfer. This can be further evidenced by calculating the Rct, and the Randle circuit (shown in SM as Fig. S1) was used to fit the data to determine the same [2]. The Rct was calculated for GCE/152.4 Ω, CDs/88.28 Ω, and nCDs/37.64 Ω.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical behavior of modified electrodes towards hydroquinone
ce:section.ce:para:
To analyze the electrochemical performance like redox reactions of electrodes such as GCE, CDs coated, and nCDs coated, in the presence of [Fe (CN)6] 3−/4− and 0.1 M KCl as an electrolyte solution with a three-electrode setup at 0.05 Vs−1 sweep rate, the CV approach was applied. when compared to coated electrode, plain GCE exhibits low oxidation and reduction peak current due to the poor transfer of electrons. The electrode modified by nCDs exhibits the greatest oxidation peak current (Ipa = 108.69 µA at a peak potential Epa = 0.32 V). The behaviour of all the electrodes is compared in the above Fig. 2 . (a & b). Because of the synergistic impact of heteroatom doping, which produces more active sites and a greater surface area in 0D material, nCDs demonstrate enhanced charge transfer kinetics. This manipulated result is the key point in the enhanced performance, and that can be proved by calculating the active surface area (ASA) by Randle’s Sevick equation (RSE) as follows:
(1)
I
p
a
= 2.69 ×
10
5
n
3 2
A
D
1 2
C
υ
1 2
Where Ipa represents the anodic peak current, ‘n’- number of electrons takes place ‘A’ denotes the effective or active surface area, the diffusion coefficient is D, C represents the concentration of ferri/ferrocyanide, and ‘υ’ is scan rate of the electrochemical reaction in mV/s. Using the above, ASA is calculated as 0.06, 0.09, and 0.13 cm2 for GCE, CDs, and nCDs, respectively, which is equivalent to doubling the geometric area's augmentation shown in Fig. 2(c)[3].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing by differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) and effect of variable scan rate
ce:section.ce:para:
From Fig. 2 (d), the DPV technique is used for the detection of 1000 µM of hydroquinone, it is chosen the pH7, 0.1 M of PBS is used as electrolyte medium. According to RSE, it is accepted that if the slope of the Ip Vs log of scan rate is close to 0.5 or less than 1, then the electro-redox reaction is mainly a diffusion-controlled process. For the above sensing is evidenced from the calibration plot (Fig. 2(f) inset) with linear regression equation calculated as
log
I
pa =
0.4 log υ + 1.2
(
R
2
= 0.99 )
Where the slope is 0.4 that is close to 0.5. It is justified that the redox reaction is reversible two electron process by the following equation,
(2)
Δ
E
P
E
pa
-
E
pc
=
59
n
m V
a t
25
°
C
Where E represents cathodic and anodic potential and number of electrons is represented by ‘n’. The obtained electron count for HQ is 1.8 that ≈ 2 which matches with the literature. The oxidative peak current of coated nCDs increases compared to GCE due to the advantages gained from higher ASA and the electrostatic interaction between the enriched surface functional groups of nCDs and HQ [4].

To find out the surface electronic charge which plays a vital role in the interaction as well as in the interfacial part, Zeta potential measurement was carried out. The Zeta potential is –22.1 mV for nCDs which becomes the main reason for the good adsorption properties in Fig. 3 .(a) [5]. The elemental composition and the presence of numerous functional groups have been proved by the full and deconvoluted spectra of XPS shown in Fig. 3 (b-e).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Concentration studies of hydroquinone using DPV
ce:section.ce:para: The LOD and Limit of Quantification (LOQ), different concentrations with a linear range of (0– 2) µM HQ were evaluated using DPV and shown in Fig. 2(e). DPV technique provides a sensitive detection limit when compared to other methods. LOD and LOQ were calculated with the equation LOD=3.3 σ/S, and LOQ=10 σ/S, sensitivity for detection of hydroquinone are 0.112 µM, 0.334 µM, and 0.02 µAµM−1 cm−2, respectively. The whole process was schematically shown in Fig. 3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Anti-Interference, Repeatability, reproducibility, and stability studies
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of a modified electrode was investigated using various interferent ions. The addition of 100 µM HQ, five times less than the interferants like Na+ions, Cl-ions, and K+ions, resulted in DPV reaction. There is no shift in potential, which obviously confirms selectivity. The CV technique was used to quantify repeatability, reproducibility, and stability illustrated in SM (Figs S1, S2, and S3). Repeatability was assessed after 20 days. Reproducibility was investigated with three different electrodes. Stability was tested for 100 cycles. The aforementioned tests clearly state nCDs are viable candidate for electrochemical sensing

In this work, nitrogen, heteroatom-doped carbon quantum dots were synthesized from the hydrothermal method from the Strychnos potatorum plant. The prepared nCDs undergo various microscopic and spectroscopic techniques. Green synthesized novel nCDs exhibit electrochemical sensing properties towards hydroquinone, an environmental contaminant. The electrochemical behaviour with HQ and its sensitivity has been examined using nCDs. Electronic surface potential by rich functional groups and the defects created by nitrogen doping are the reasons behind the enhanced electron transfer, and the higher ASA being a zero-dimensional element improves the electrocatalytic activity in the direct oxidation of HQ with LOD of 0.112 µM.

Nithya Selvaraju: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Akash Sivanandan: Validation, Investigation, Data curation. Gunasekaran Venugopal: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are thankful to Central University of Tamil Nadu for providing the experimental lab facility. The authors acknowledge the financial support from UGC-DAE-CSR through a Collaborative Research Scheme (CRS) project number CRS/2022-23/01/673.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Electrode preparation, Equivalent circuit, Anti-Interference, Repeatability, reproducibility, stability studies, and comparison table. Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matlet.2024.137022.

ce:section.ce:para:
